title: Rootless podman causes slow behaviour for nginx subdomain reverse proxy. Running as root restores performance.
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

Nginx has poor performance when running as rootless for certain a application under subdomain redirect. Loading time is around 20-30 seconds. When ran as root, loading time is cut down to 5-6 seconds. I initially filed a bug report at jellyfin due to faulty reasoning, to save myself typing out all my findings here, please visit the initial issue I opened: https://github.com/jellyfin/jellyfin-web/issues/1515

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Setup nginx and jellyfin using podman, you can see my config here to get started quickly: https://github.com/jellyfin/jellyfin-web/issues/1515

2. Observe the difference in performance when running with and without sudo using your web browser performance inspector.

I went into quite a lot of detail here: https://github.com/jellyfin/jellyfin-web/issues/1515


**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
(paste your output here)
```

comments.body: I suspect that this is slirp4netns. Rootless networking uses slirp4netns instead of creating an interface in a network bridge, which is a sort of manual bridge, tunnelling traffic from the host into the container. We can't really do this differently, as rootless Podman doesn't have the necessary permissions to set up a network bridge like root Podman does. If you require further performance, you could consider disabling network isolation with `--net=host` which should remove slirp4netns from the equation.

Any advice on how to troubleshoot slirp4netns? I want to see exactly why it's not handling the GET requests properly and taking 5000ms to respond on certain GET requests. I'd like to understand why there is no performance impact for nextcloud, but a massive one for jellyfin.

> Is this related?
> https://github.com/rootless-containers/slirp4netns/blob/v1.1.4/slirp4netns.1.md#bugs

I'd like to test this but I'm really not familiar at all with namespaces, I don't understand how to run this first command:

```
nsenter -t $(cat /tmp/pid) -U -n -m
```
What's up with the target flag being some temp file? Shouldn't the flags following also have some sort of input?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

>  I don't understand how to run this first command:

Podman doesn't write slirp4netns PID to any file, so please substitute `cat /tmp/pid` with `pgrep slirp4netns`
Since we got no response, I am going to close this issue, Reopen if more information is available.

[I ran into this issue myself](https://www.reddit.com/r/linuxadmin/comments/o6e1x6/performance_issues_when_running_nginx_reverse/). This issue only appears when running both containers on the same host. Running the same containers on two separate machines works fine.

I tried to change tcp_rmem but running:

`nsenter -t $(cat /tmp/pid) -U -n -m`

as @AkihiroSuda suggested gives me the following error:

`nsenter: reassociate to namespace 'ns/net' failed: Operation not permitted`

Distro: Arch
Podman: 3.2.1-1
slirp4netns: 1.1.11-1

Adding --network slirp4netns:mtu=1500 to the podman run command for the nginx container appears to fix the issue.

@AkihiroSuda I messed up the c&p. I ran it with your pgrep slirp4netns substitution.

Running 
`nsenter -t $(pgrep slirp4netns) -U --preserve-credentials -n -m`
Gives me the same error message as before.
You have to specify a PID that is associated with the child network namespace. i.e., a PID of container. (`podman inspect <CONTAINER>`)
